text: OF AUSTRALIA, 615 [HIGH COURT OF AUSTRALIA.) PATERSON APPELLANT; DEFENDANT, AND MeNAGHTEN: fe . RESPONDENT. PLAINTIFF, ON APPEAL FROM THE SUPREME COURT OF NEW SOUTH WALES. fe—Sepriration deed—Restraint on anticipation—Eifect of clause 'on revoeability of contract—Waiver of condition precedent—Eyuitable plea— Accord and satisfaction, Sypyey, the Supreme Court, brought an action to recover from the appellant arrears staat alleged to be due under a covenant in a deed of separation by which the | —— The respondent, as trustee of a chose in action vested in him by an order of appellant covenanted to pay to a trustee a certain sum perannum by quarterly Grtith C.J. instalments, upon trust for his wife for her separate use without power of Connor JJ. suticipation. 'The appellant pleaded inter alia, an equitable plea alleging that by 4 clause of the deed it was provided that under certain cireumstances, upon the performance by the appellant of certain conditions as to giving notice, the deed The plea alleged should be considered as at an end and its covenants voi further that the circumstances contemplated in the said clause had arisen, that the wife had waived the performance of the condition as to notice, and that the deed was therefore at an end, and its covenants void ; and that the 'appellant, before any of the moneys claimed became due, ceased to pay the sum covenanted to be paid, and paid other sums to the wife and entered into other arrangements with her from time to time, and that these moneys were opal and arrangements entered into by the appellant on the faith and underSanding that the arrangement in the deed was at an end and the covenants 'oid, and his wife accepted these moneys and entereil into these arrangements on the same faith and understanding, and by her conduct both she and the Fespoudent were estopped from setting up and suing upon the deed, Issue Was joined upon the pleas.

Trappeared at the trial that at the date of the alleged notice, one quarterly instalment was due and unpaid. 'The jury found specially that the wife had H.C. or A. 1905. — Parenson MoNacures. HIGH COURT 7 waived the performance of the condition as to notice, and als ta, accepted the moneys paid and the other arrangements made after te of the waiver, as alleged in the plea. ' Held, that the plea must be construed not as merely setting upa ys by the wife of the condition as to notice, but as further alleging that on occasion when a payment was made under the substituted arrangement ty wife accepted the payment and the fresh arrangement as a antsiatio of instalments then due, and agreed not to insist upon the stipulation gx notice or to set up the deed as creating a subs : { ion op wife with respect to income actually accrued due, the plea disclosed a gol defence in Equity as to all the instalments sued for except the first; and 'That, upon the findings of the jury, the defence had been substan proved. k; Hood Barrs v. Heriot, A.C., 174, followed. " Semble, that but for the equitable nature of the interests involved, thi would also have been a good defence at common laws, by way of accord satisfaction.

An executory contract to which a married woman is. party, and whith does not amount to a complete gift of property, is not made irrevocable by the mere fact that it contains a clause in restraint of anticipation with rein to her rights under the contract. 7 Semble, therefore, that in a separation deed by which no property isassguel such a clause would not prevent the wife from rescinding or releasing the deel, or waiving her rights under it. Decision of the Supreme Court, MeNaghten v. Paterson, 5 8B. (N.S.1.), 90, reversed, and judgment of Darley C.J. restored.

Appeat from a decision of the Supreme Court of New South Wales. The following statement of the facts is taken from the judgment of Grifith CJ. :— This action was brought by the respondent as trustee ofa chase in action which had been vested in him by an order of the Supreme Court under the Trustee Act (N.S.W.) 1898, to recover arrears of money covenanted to be paid by the appellant under deed of separation, dated 2nd April, 1894, and made between the appellant of the first part, M. S.

Paterson, his wife, of the second part, and J.8, Gill, who resided out of New South Wales, of the third part 'The respondent, as assignee of the chose in action, took it, of course, with all its equities, 'The deed, after stipulations that i should be lawful for the wife to live apart from her husband and H. C. oF A. tochoose her residence wherever she might think fit, provided 19%. fiat sueh residence did not prevent her compliance with elause 6 pasyaaox of the deed, and that neither should molest the other or take any y4.5,"" suey, "eps to compel a return to cohabitation, contained in clause 3. a = — covenant by the appellant to pay to Gill during the joint lives of himself and his wife, subject to clause 4, the sum of £400, payable quarterly on the first days of January, April, July, and October, jn each year, "upon trust for the said M. S.

Paterson for her pirate use and she shall not have power to anticipate the same." Clause 4 of the deed is as follows :—" In the event of the said H Paterson," (the appellant) "at any time after the expiration of twelve months from the date hereof considering that the said amount in the third clause mentioned should by reason of the diminution of his income or otherwise be reduced, he shall give totice thereof to the said J. S. Gill and in the event of no agreement being arrived at between all the parties as to the amount to be thereafter paid by the said H.
Paterson to the said J. S. Gill within one month after notice of his intention to pay such ~ reduced amount has been received by the said J.

S. Gill from the stil H. Paterson then the arrangement herein contained shall be considered as at an end and the covenants and agreement lerein contained shall become void and not hereafter binding upon the parties." Clauses 6 and 7 stipulated that the wife should have sole ustody and control of the child of the marriage, and of his aducation and bringing up till he attained 21, without any interference on the part of the husband, with a proviso that the child should spend every alternate Sunday with his father in Sydney extept on two occasions in the year, when his mother was to be atliberty to take the child away from Sydney for periods not txceeding six weeks.

By clause 8 Gill eovenanted to indemnify the appellant against any claims for his wife's debts 'The instalment of £100 payable on Ist April, 1895, was not Paid by the appellant. On 26th March his solicitors had written toa Mr. Hamilton, a solicitor who had acted as Gill's agent to tesive the money payable under the deed of separation, to ask ithe was prepared to accept service of a notice in terms of clause H.C. oF A 1905. 'PATERSON c MoNacures.

HIGH COURT 4 of the deed and to waive the necessity of a notice being sent t Gill, On 3rd April an interview took place between Ha and the appellant's solicitor, at which Hamilton, acting, asi deposed, on the authority of Mrs. Paterson, agreed to waive th question of notice to the trustee. Negotiations then took ply between them as to the reduced amount to be paid to her, b they failed to come to an agreement. 'These negotiations had of course, no effect upon the claim for the £100 which had al accrued due.

Mrs. Paterson then instituted a suit for ju separation and obtained an order for alimony pendente lite, suit was dismissed on the merits in November, 1895, In January, 1896, she applied for an order for maintenane under the Deserted Wives and Children's Act, but her complain was dismissed. Further negotiations then took place betwes the husband and wife through their respective solicitors, with th result that fresh arrangements were made from time to time ss to the allowance to be paid to her by the appellant, and as to custody of the child, and the moneys payable under the fresh arrangements were from time to time paid to and accepted the wife.

On 5th March, 1897, an order was made by the Supreme Cou by consent, upon an application by the appellant for a writ o habeas corpus against the wife in respect of the child, by wl the conditions of the deed as to the access of the appellant to child were materially varied, and it was ordered that the appellast should pay the wife £15 a month by way of maintenance for the wife and child (subject to diminution in certain events), nl should also pay the child's school fees at a rate to be agree upon between the parties' solicitors. 'This order was to be it foree for two years from its date. On 3rd May, 1899, another order was made by Cohen J. al upon an application for a writ of habeas corpus by the appellanh whereby the eustody of the child was given to the appellant full directions as to his future edueation at the appellants expe The child was to spend the first half of his vacations with father, and the second half with his mother. No limit assigned to the duration of this order, and it is still in foree The plaintiff's declaration in the action alleged that the aP oLR] OF AUSTRALIA. 619 by deed of 2nd April, 1894, covenanted to pay Gill the sum of H. ©. o A. 4400 per annum by quarterly instalments, but did not do so, that pete 'he balance of £1879 9s. 4d. remained due, and that the right to pasos 'ae for and recover that sum had been vested in the plaintiff "Qndit was given for the sums actually paid by the appellant.

The appellant pleaded denying the existence of the alleged 'bbt, and also pleaded an equitable plea, alleging that the deed sel upon was a deed of separation, Gill being a trustee as therein entioned. 'The plea then set out the fourth clause of the deed snd proceeded :— _ "And the appellant says that, after the expiration of the said twelve months from the date of the said deed, and before any 'portion of the said sum of £1879 9s, 4d., herein sued for beeame 'ue under the said covenant of the said deed, the defendant considered that the said amount in the said third clause of the said deed mentioned, should, by reason of the diminution of his income and otherwise, be reduced, and the defendant says that the said J. S. Gill was a trustee under the said deed for the sid M.S, Paterson, and was by the said deed to receive the said moneys for her the said M.

Paterson was and is solely entitled to the said moneys to be paid under the covenant in the said deed in the declaration mentioned and svel upon, and this action is now brought by the plaintiff as trustee for and for the sole benefit of the said M.S, Paterson ; and thedefendant says that he gave notice of the fact that he considered that the said amount in the said third clause of the said deed mentioned should for the reason aforesaid be reduced to the 'olistor acting for the said M. S. Gill; and the defendant says that no agreement was arrived at between the said parties the itfendant and the said J. S.

Gill and the said M.S. Paterson, and entered into other ara was at an end, and the said covenant void; and the said M Paterson accepted the said moneys and entered into the said oth arrangements upon the same faith and understanding, and by her conduct from now setting up and suing upon the s deed." On these pleas issue was joined.

It did not appear on the pleadings that the money was to be paid to Gill for the s use of the wife without power of anticipation. At the trial the jury, in answer to questions left to them by the learned Chief Justice, found that Mrs. Paterson had notice to Hamilton as a good notice under clause 4, and hal exonerated the appellant from giving notice to Gill.

They found that she "accepted the moneys paid and other arrang ments made since April, 1895, on the faith and understandi that the arrangement in the deed was at an end and the covennt therein contained void." On these findings the learned Chief Justice directed a verdi for the plaintiff for £100, reserving leave to the plaintiff to mo to increase the verdict to £1879 9s. 4d. A rule to increase th verdict accordingly was afterwards made absolute by the Bull Court: MeNughten v. Paterson (1). 'The appeal was from this rule.

Gordon KC. and Rolin, for the appellant. The orders at Supreme Court under which the respondent was appol! (1) 5 S.R. (N.S.W.), 90. OF AUSTRALIA. 621 ive the moneys and to sue for them are invalid, and he is not H.C. or A. proper party to sue.

Sec. 38, sub-secs. (c) and (/) of the 105 1 Act 1898 applies only to the case of a bare trustee. Gill pantaoy not a bare trustee, but was bound to indemnify, MeNaghten 4.4." yy 'd not be sued upon the covenant by Gill to indemnify. =a {Gnrrira C.J—As the pleadings stand, if the orders could be leat all, they must be taken to be good. The section seems toapply to this case.] 'The principle of Jorden v.
Money (1) upon which the Supreme Court relied, does not apply. Here a waiver is alleged. The ife had power to waive the notice.

Even rights given by Act Parliament may be waived. The deed here contains no provison as to any particular form of notice, and the notice necesunder clause 4 was waived. If the notice had been given as yuired by that clause, the deed would have been at anend.
The of the wife amounted to a representation that the deed was anend. She treated notice to herself as notice to Gill. The jury have found both waiver and estoppel against the wife.

All ihe allegations in the equitable plea have been found in the sypellant's favour. 'The evidence shows that arrangements were made and proceedings taken by both parties inconsistent with their rights under the deed. It is not open to the wife or the respondent to contend now that the rights of the wife under the eed remain unchanged. [He referred to Chadwick v. Manning Q); Earl Beauchamp v. Wynn (3); Selwyn v. Garfit (4); In re Thompson and Holt (5); Watts v.
Green (9); Encyclopedia of the Laws of England, sub. Waiver and Acquiescence.] 'The doctrine of restraint on anticipation cannot be applied to such a deed as this. The authority of Bateman v.

Fuber (10), is tut disputed, but it applies only to separate property settled on a ied woman. 'The sepatation deed constitutes the wife a dome sole as regards the benefits under it: Cahill v. Cahill (11) (7) 3 Macq. HL.

They are of a 'ehnical character, and have always been given a definite naning.

This is an instance of the very case which the doctrine s intended to meet, viz., to protect the wife against the influmee of her husband: In re Ridley; Buckton v. Hay (5); In re Tardon's Trusts (6). Heath v.

OF AUSTRALIA. 625 (He was stopped on this point.} H.C. or A. 'The restraint on anticipation does not prevent a married woman Lal jou rescinding a contract. It only applies to money payable to Patensox weunder it, She cannot forestall it, but she may re-contract. Me NAcnres.

Tere is no case in which the restraint has been applied to any—— 'ting which was not in the nature of property. It is to protect le property of the separate estate. But it does not apply to a contract, like a separation deed, which the wife has clearly to break in one way, i.e., by re-cohabitation.
Birmingham Fuelsion Money Society v. Lane (1), only applies where the deed siuittedly subsists, it is no authority for the contention that the del is irrevocable. Heath v.

Wickhum (2) is fatal to the sspondent's contention on this point. He is not enforeing the "venant, but is suing as trustee for past income already due to fiewife. The restraint ceases immediately the moneys become pyible: Hood Burrs v.

Heriot (3). For these sums, as they 'frame due, there has been an accord and satisfaction from time fotime. 'The wife agreed on each occasion in return for further wiventages to forego the amounts not paid, and the provisions "ofthe deed, and accepted the smaller sum. Different arrange'ments were made on many matters, altogether inconsistent with tiedeed, and both parties accepted them as part of the consideratim for the new arrangement. (GurrrrH C.J. referred to Goddard v.

O'Brien (4)} That is the gist of the plea, and the third finding of the jury is slstantially a finding that the plea has been made out. The 'tie, having allowed the appellant all these years to pay moneys tul to alter his position on the footing that the deed was at 'mend, is now debarred by her laches and acquiescence from siting it up. 'There were, therefore, faets alleged and proved Thich amount to an absolute discharge of the liability under the 'el. It was treated as applying only to what took pln in 1895, 4 [Grirrirn CJ. referred to Hobson v.

Couley (2)] That was a case of substituted agreement, not of accord aul satisfaction. A defence of accord before breach would be met | the restraint on anticipation. If the Court decides in favour of the appellant on this Day v.

MeLea (4)] GrireirH C.J. [His Honor, haying stated the facts as al set out, continued]: Owen J. in his judgment referred to the known rule laid down in Jorden v. Money (5); and Chad Manning (6), that in order to create an estoppel a represent (4) 22. B.D., 610. 6 , 185, (6) AG, 281.

RJ) OF AUSTRALIA, 627 tbe of an existing fact and not of a mere intention; and he H.C. or A. wught that the waiver of notiee set up by the appellant 5 unted to no more than an expression of intention by the wife pyrsesox to avail herself of the failure to give notice and not to set Up sey," ypu, ihe deed as valid. If this were the true effect of a waiver this —— ing would be conclusive. But, as I will presently show, the ine of waiver rests on other grounds.

The learned Judge dealt with the power of the wife to waive the notice, and wht that she had no such power by reason of the restraint on ticipation, referring to the case of Bateman vy. Fuber (1) in which Lindley MLR. said: "A married woman cannot by hook or ly erook—by any device, even by her own fraud (the eases go that length)—deprive herself of the protection which the restraint 'ay anticipation throws around her." If the doctrine of restraint 'manticipation applies to this case, this reasoning is also unanswerth, The learned Judge also thought that the wife's waiver could not in any case bind the trustee. A.
H. Simpson J. came to the same conclusion, but with reluct* 'mee. He rested his judgment entirely on the doctrine of restraint 'mm anticipation.
Pring J. thought that the trustee himself could not have "waived the notice. Iwill deal first with the question of restraint on anticipation. In In ve Ridley, Buckton v.

Hay (2) the doctrine was considered amd expounded by Jessel M.R. He said: " Now, it is necessary toconsider what the meaning of a restraint on anticipation is, for with the exception of a single observation in one of the authoritiesto which I will refer presently, the point does not seem to lave been discussed at all. "In the first place, the law of this country says that all property stall be alienable; but there has been one exception to that gueral law, for restraint on anticipation or alienation was allowed inthe case of a married woman. That was purely an equity tketrine, the invention of the Chancellors, and is, as I have said, mexeeption to the general law which says that property shall wt be inalienable.

That exception was justified on the ground that it was the only way, or at least the best way, of giving (Y/ 1. Ch,, 144, at p. 149. )) 11. Ch, D., 645, at p. 648. 628 HIGH COURT La ied A. property to a married woman. Tt was considered that to gi J. her without such a restraint would be, practically, to give j Parmmsos her husband, and therefore, to prevent this, a condition yy allowed to be imposed restraining her from anticipating her ings, and thus fettering the free alienation of her property, "That ground I must assume to be correct. The result, then fore, was that the exception to the general law was in favour of married women, to enable them to enjoy their property, "Then there was another rule, also inyented by the Chaneslan, in analogy to the common law.

That was an invention of different kind from the other, and was this time in favour alienation and not against it. The law does not recognize positions which would practically make property inalienable fr ever. Contingent remainders were introduced, which had th effect of rendering property inalienable. The doctrine of em tingent remainders was discussed by the Chancellors, who held that a remainder depending upon what was called a possibility on a possibility was contrary to the common law. 'That was wholesome rule, only it was considered that it did not go fr enough. 'The result was that the Chancellors established this rule in favour of alienation, that property could not be tied up longer than for a life in being and twenty-one years after. is called the rule against perpetuities.

This rule, therefore, ws established directly in favour of alienation ; it merely carried ont the principle of law that property is alienable. Similarly inthe case of executory interests, the law put a limit or fetter upon the testamentary power. The theory of both rules is, however, th same, namely, that property is alienable, though it may be male inalienable to a certain extent and in a peculiar way ' "The question is, whether the restraint on alienation should nt be allowed within certain limits under the one rule aswell under the other. The first exception is a clear and manifest exception to the general law, which says that property shall be alienable ; the question is, whether there should not be a similar exception to that branch of the general law which says that property shall not be inalienable beyond a life in being aol twenty-one years after.

But this question does not appest tome to have been well weighed or considered." 'The learned Masi v. 'MoNacures. Griich C.. OLR] OF AUSTRALIA. inst perpetuities must prevail against the rule of restraint on jcipation. iuris may rescind or release an exeeutory contract comes in 'confict with the rule of restraint on anticipation.

It is clear that 'the doctrine by which the separate estate of a married woman 'ould be made inalienable was, when first invented by Lord Thurlow [See note to Pybus v. Smith (1)], a rule attaching an incident to property, i.c., to property of a specific kind, namely, gparate estate already existing. It could not, at that time, have 'tended to make an executory contract by a married woman not 'hating to existing separate estate irrevocable, because at that time shecould not make any such contract at all.

The proposal, therefor, to apply the doctrine to the case of an executory contract ofa married woman not relating to existing property, and not lating to property at all, except so far as the chose in action qeated by the contract itself is property, is an extension of the signal doctrine to a ease which it was not originally intended toever. Now, itis, in general, as much an incident of a contract between persons swi juris that it may be rescinded or released as 'tisan incident of property that it may be alienated, and under 'the Married Women's Property Act married women are persons ui juris for all purposes' except as to separate estate subject to 'straint on anticipation. 'I'wo cases were cited to us in which, 'against judgment creditors, moneys payable under a deed of "aration to a trustee for the benefit of the wife with restraint 'M™ anticipation were assumed to be within the doctrine. The 'Joint was not raised in either case, But it does not follow 'that, beeause the doctrine should be held to apply to such a case, th: power of a married woman to rescind an existing contract: ty be destroyed by a stipulation in the contract that it shall 'efor her benefit without power of anticipation.

The distinetim between a completed gift of property and a gift which is 'aly complete by way of contract was adverted to by Cotton LJ. in Nicol v. Nicol (2). No authority was cited to us to show that (1) 3 Bro, C.C., 340 ; and see 1 Ves., 189. (2) 31 Ch.

D., 624, at p. 527. You a, 43 { the Rolls then gave his reasons for holding that the rule H.C. or A. 1905. 2S PATERSON imi] msiderations arise when the rule thi i 3 Sinilar co at a person si ox Seay, Grime 0.3. H.C. or A. an executory contract to which a married woman is pay, 1905.

Grifith C4. "contract. Very singular consequences would follow from y HIGH COURT which does not amount to a complete gift of existing pup can be made irrevocable by the use of the magie formula yi holding. The suggested doctrine is clearly not partof theo doctrine as laid down by Lord Thurlow and as expounded i Tullett v.

I do not wish, wi necessity, to express a final conclusion upon a new point of 9 much importance in the law of married women's property Prefer to base my decision upon a ground whieh is entirely from that difficulty. ' Equitable pleas are allowed by sec. 95 of the New South Wal OF AUSTRALIA Common Law Procedure Act 1899, which repeats the words "of tie earlier Statute first authorizing equitable pleas. The ition provides that "the defendant in any action in which sfjudgment were obtained he would be entitled to relief against "ch judgment on equitable grounds may plead the facts which intitle him to such relief by way of defence." It has been held "hat the facts alleged in the plea must be such as would entitle the defendant to a perpetual and unconditional injunction against " fhedefendant in a Court of Equity. The test to be applied to "the plea is therefore this—does it set out facts which would jmify a Court of Equity in granting a perpetual and uncontitional injunction against the plaintiff proceeding upon a judgnent for her claim ?

It is plain from the form of the plea that it was intended to put forward as the ground of defence an qquitable rescission of the deed. For the purposes of this judgnent, [assume that such a rescission would be contrary to the law of restraint against anticipation, and that, if the plea rested upon that ground only, it would be no answer to the action. But atthe end of the plea, no doubt, as showing a consideration for the waiver alleged, and a change of the defendant's position on "the faith thereof, there occurs the following statement of facts :— "And the defendant paid other moneys " (meaning moneys other thn those covenanted to be paid) "to the said Mary Stewart Paterson, and entered into other arrangements with her from time to time, and the said moneys were so paid, and the said "amangements entered into by the defendant on the faith and idertaking that the arrangement in the said deed was at an end, and the said covenant void, and the said Mary Stewart Paterson _ 'teepted the said moneys and entered into the said other arrrangetents on the same faith and undertaking, and is now estopped tyher conduct, and the plaintiff is estopped by her conduct from wow setting up and suing upon the said deed." The allegation sto estoppel i is merely a statement of an inference in law.

It is also said that he waiver cannot bind Gill. It is not necessary that Gill should le bound for the purpose of an injunction on the facts then underem sideration. The jurisdiction of an Equity Court is over the pena and it will sometimes restrain a party from inequitably taki advantage of a provision in a deed which for all other purposes,aul in respect of other parties, is treated as being in full fore.

Mrs. Paterson or her trustee were inequitably seeking to en the deed, the Court would restrain her or her trustee from pr ceeding. : The principle upon which the Court of Equity will res the inequitable use of a provision in a deed is well Mustratel by Wing v. Harvey (2).

That was a suit against an insurance society for payment of money on a life policy taken out iy i assured at the instance of his ereditor and assigned to the latter One eondition of the policy was that, if the assured shold & beyond the limits of Europe without the licence of the dieears (1) A.C., 174. (2) 23 L.J., Ch., a. 4 OLR) OF AUSTRALIA. 'be poliey should be void, and all money' paid thereunder should "joe forfeited. 'The assured, without he licence of the directors, "fid.g> beyond the Timits of Europe, to Canada, lived there for = years, and.died there. 'The society set up this breach of eon'jition as a defence. Tt was proved by the plaintiff that the repreeatative of the society, who had been in the habit of receiving 'the premiums while the assured was in England, was informed of "the breach of condition by the agent of the assured, and, in answer 'jo the question whether it would be safe to continue paying premiums under the circumstances, stated that the policy would ie perfectly good, if the premiums were regularly paid. On this ssiranee, made at the time of each payment, the premiums were rgnlarly paid until the death of the assured.

The Court, Knight Bruce LJ. and Turner LJ., holding that the society was bound 'yy the acts of their representative, decided that the defence was inefectual, as there had been a waiver of the forfeiture. Knight Bruce Li, in the course of the argument, states the principle of the decision (1): "The party pays and the agent receives the premiums upon the faith and condition that the policies are to be sidered as valid and subsisting; and the argument is, that although the money was paid on those express conditions, the peson paying it is to be in the same position as if he had paid it w0onditionally. How can he be now reinstated in his position ?

Ithe had been informed in 1835," (the date of the breach of condition) "that the forfeiture would be insisted on, he might have fusured the life at another office: not so now, the life having topped." The principle of that decision may well be applied in thisease. 'The jury have found that Mrs. Paterson accepted the ttice to her solicitor, instead of to Gill, as a good notice to "termine the deed; that she exonerated the defendant from giving notice to Gill; that she accepted the moneys paid as -tlieplea alleges from time to time, and the other arrangements tale since April, 1895, on the faith and understanding that thearangement in the deed was at an end, and the covenant lor payment therein void. A waiver, a statement, a promise 'tay be inferred from conduct as well as from words, The series anangements and proceedings that took place after the notice (1) 23 L.d.; Ch., 501, atp. 514, You. 1, 44 H.C. or A. 1905, PATERSON v.

HIGH COURT to Mrs, Paterson's solicitor were absolutely inconsistent ith respective rights of Mrs. Paterson and her husband under g deed, and it must be taken that the payments from time to ag, made by the defendant to his wife from that time on for gue eight years were made upon the express condition under was at an end. Upon the faith of that condition he altered 4 position, omitted to give the notice to Gill, which he othe would have given, entered into arrangements, and made payment solely on the faith of the deed being at an end.

That al position and those benetits are the consideration for the waiverdf. her rights by Mrs. Paterson. She has received the benefit of the consideration, the defendant cannot be reinstated in the posit of advantage as to the notice which he gave up, and, in so farasa_ Court of Equity could interfere, it would not permit the p who has received these benefits as a consideration for waivi rights, to still insist upon their enforcement.

Assuming that Court of Equity would not, upon the facts, allow the deed by reason of the restraint against anticipation to be treated as at an end for all purposes, I have no doubt that it would granta perpetual and unconditional injunction against Mrs, Patersmls assertion of any rights under the deed in respect of the mon accrued due at the commencement of the action on the gro that she had, for valuable consideration, waived those rights Holding these views, I am of opinion that the facts stated in the equitable plea, and found by the jury in the defendant's favour are a complete answer to the plaintiff's claim, except in respet the £100 for which the verdict has been entered. I agree wi my learned brother the Chief Justice that the plaintiff is mt entitled to have the verdict increased, as ordered by the Suprem Court, and that the appeal must be upheld.
